messages.text: What custom tool would you like to build for this space? (e.g., Kanban board, project dashboard, asset tracker)
make a simple kanban board to track my teams work

**Defining Scope and Simplicity**

I'm zeroing in on the core of the request: a *simple* Kanban board. The guiding principle is radical simplicity—delivering precisely what the user wants without feature creep. I'm focusing on defining the absolute minimum viable feature set.


**Refining Aesthetic and Data**

I'm now honing in on the design aesthetic: clean, modern, and extremely simple. I'm focusing on functional code, rejecting placeholders. Based on the provided files, I'll ingest actual data from the "Notes - Ollie daily sync" file to populate the Kanban board with real tasks, like "Coding Agent Mike Carcasole" and dates like "Jul 7, 2026."


**Prioritizing Real Data**

I'm making sure this Kanban board feels relevant to their *current* projects. Pulling real tasks directly from their "Notes - Ollie daily sync" file is crucial. I'll use tasks like "Coding Agent Mike Carcasole" and "UX/UI Refinements Simon Sok" to give an immediate sense of functionality. It is very important to get a sense of speed in this project.


**Prioritizing Immediate Output**

I'm now laser-focused on efficiency, following the hard rule to avoid any exploratory commands or file system interaction. The key is to deliver the complete `index.html` code directly in the markdown block, skipping any intermediate steps or testing. This means I'm skipping ALL file system-related calls.


**Integrating Real-time Synchronization**

I've got a fantastic, speedy update! The lightbulb just went on regarding real-time collaboration. The Kanban board, designed for team work, inherently needs synchronization. I'm excited to integrate the `/api/sync/:envId/:key` API to make column and card updates real-time. This includes using `window.__sandboxEnvId` for `EnvId` and `"ollie_kanban_board_state"` as the sync key. I'll also be sure to implement the POST and GET fetch endpoints: `POST /api/sync/${envId}/${syncKey}` with `{ data: ... }` and `GET /api/sync/${envId}/${syncKey}`, returning `{ data: ... }`. The WDS will be carefully reviewed.


**Incorporating New Style Elements**

I'm now integrating the provided style guide. I'm focusing on incorporating the specific CSS variables, color palette, and font choices to ensure the Kanban board adheres to the desired aesthetic. The goal is to build a very simple but crisp design. The font is Google Sans and Google Sans Text and I am including those fonts.



**Implementing Minimal Styling**

I am now focusing on implementing the minimal visual elements: flat background colors, rounded borders using `rounded-lg`, and outline borders. I am implementing generous padding as well. I'm avoiding gradients, drop shadows, and any other visual distractions, keeping the design clean and simple. I have seeded the Kanban board columns with actual tasks pulled directly from the provided files to ensure the board is realistic.


**Designing Application Structure**

I'm now zeroing in on the fundamental structure. I'm building out the basic UI elements: a prominent header reading "Project Ollie Tracker," and the three key columns: "To Do," "In Progress," and "Done." Each column will have a straightforward text input for adding new cards directly, avoiding any clunky dialog boxes. The user will be able to either drag and drop cards or use control buttons.


**Adding Drag-and-Drop Functionality**

I'm now implementing native vanilla JS HTML5 drag-and-drop. I'm focusing on making cards draggable with cursor styling. Each card will have a delete icon. I'm also implementing the simple text input at the bottom of each column to add tasks. I'm implementing a clean and simple minimalist input box: `<input type="text" placeholder="Add task..." ...>`. The design will be simple, with a 3-column container.

I'll be sure to verify the state synchronization implementation as well!


**Updating Data Elements**

I'm now focusing on integrating the provided sample data into the Kanban board's columns. I am using the new data to make sure that the board is relevant and functional, as well as testing to make sure the app correctly loads and displays. I am carefully aligning the sample data's tasks with "To Do," "In Progress," and "Done" columns.


**Implementing Color Schemes**

I'm now integrating light and dark mode sensing. I'll use `@media (prefers-color-scheme: dark)` to apply the appropriate color variables, ensuring the Kanban board adapts to the user's workspace environment. I'll define CSS variables, like `--md-primary` and `--md-surface`, for a modern, clean, and professional design. I will use the provided color code, including the `surface` and `outline` values.



**Refining Workspace Integration**

I'm now integrating dynamic workspace name display into the header, using `window.__sandboxWorkspaceName` for the workspace label. I'll dynamically generate a sleek header that reads "Workspace Name Board" or "Project Ollie Board" if the workspace name is not provided. I'm focusing on the elegant structure of the HTML elements for each column: a title, card count, card container, cards, and input.


**Adding Task Form Functionality**

I'm now implementing the task input form. I'm prioritizing extreme minimalism: a clean, inline input element with either a check button or 'Enter' key integration for task addition. The design is intended to be intuitive.


**Implementing Drag-and-Drop**

I'm implementing drag-and-drop now with native JavaScript, focusing on seamless user experience. I'm building visual cues for dragover events, including a subtle border highlight. When a card is dropped, I will handle the `dragend` event and synchronize the state. I am ensuring that I have implemented clean, bulletproof, native Drag-and-Drop JS.


**Adding Card Reordering**

I'm now incorporating card reordering within columns, as a core piece of task management. I'm focusing on ensuring seamless movement between columns. I will be sure to support the `moveCard` function for column movement. I am also planning on adding an alternative method, so that even a nested iframe will allow editing.


**Adding Move Buttons**

I'm now integrating ultra-clean, minimalist "◀" and "▶" move buttons that will appear on hover. These arrows enable single-click column transfers, ensuring optimal usability. I'm focusing on ensuring both drag-and-drop and intuitive click/touch controls coexist seamlessly. I will check the font, background, and other elements, too.

\n\n
I'm ensuring that I'm also implementing the buttons with robust event handling, as well as checking the appropriate font family. The intention here is to enable intuitive, yet simple, card movement between columns, which is a core feature for task management. I am still keeping the layout simple and solid.


**Implementing Premium Aesthetic**

I'm now focusing on the meticulous design of individual Kanban board cards, drawing inspiration from Google Workspace. I'm leveraging CSS variables for dynamic styling. I've focused on ensuring the visual elements match the Google Workspace card design and spacing. I'm implementing a minimalist navigation bar to indicate real-time synchronization status with a green dot for "Synced" and a gray dot for "Offline."


**Finalizing Kanban Board Structure**

I'm now putting the finishing touches on the entire `index.html` structure. It's now optimized for responsiveness across devices and will automatically respond to light/dark mode settings. I'm ensuring seamless real-time synchronization with the Workspace Synchronization API, with a fallback to `localStorage`.


**Designing Input Stability**

I'm now prioritizing input field stability during data synchronization. I will implement mechanisms to preserve input text during real-time updates, focusing on partial DOM re-rendering and local state management for a seamless user experience. I am keeping the UX solid.


**Optimizing Rendering and State**

I'm now implementing the optimized DOM rendering technique. I will maintain the column structure and input elements as static to preserve user input focus. I'll focus on re-rendering only the `.column-cards` element to improve efficiency. I'm also ensuring the initial state uses workspace data.


Here is the complete, high-performance Kanban board designed explicitly around **Robert Murdock's Polaris Design System** and **Material Design 3 (M3)** specifications. 

It is pre-seeded with actual context from your project (Project Ollie / Project Friday) extracted from your sync documents (including items like Mike's coding agent, Cowboy's quality bash, and Mirjam's UXR track). It features automatic Light/Dark mode syncing and real-time multiplayer synchronization through the built-in flat-file synchronization framework.

sandboxFiles.name: [UX Research plan] Project Ollie: Baseline Workflow & Agent Delegation
sandboxFiles.content:
Core Objective
To map the data trails, tool ecosystems, and context-sharing habits of knowledge workers in the real world, as well as their desires and expectations regarding AI delegation.
Participants
* Location & Availability: Must currently live in the U.S. and be able to attend a 90-minute, in-person study session at the NYC Google Office between July 6th and July 24th with their own laptop.1
* Company Profile: Employed at a private educational institute or a commercial business/organization (excluding public education, nonprofits, and government).1
* Software & Tool Usage:
   * Their company must use Google Workspace (Gmail, Drive, Docs, or Calendar) with official work email addresses.1
   * The "actual work" they do must involve co-editing shared documents and spreadsheets (such as Google Docs or Sheets). Users who primarily manage tasks in dedicated project management tools (like Jira, Asana, or Trello) are disqualified.1
* Role & Authority: Must have a role in their business's decision to purchase digital tools and software (either as a sole decision-maker, part of a collective group, or as an influencer).1
* Collaboration Level: They must spend at least half or more of their work week collaborating with others on projects, tasks, or shared goals.1
Details here: Ollie current needs and painpoints 


Research Questions 
* RQ1 (The Fragmented Truth): What does a knowledge worker's true "to-do list" actually look like across formal tools (Jira, Asana) and informal channels (Slack stars, open browser tabs, mental notes)?
* RQ2 (Context Breadcrumbs): When a user moves a task forward, what specific artifacts (links, documents, specific chat sentences) represent the "proof of work" that Ollie will eventually need to ingest?
* RQ3 (The Translation Gap): How do users naturally summarize a complex day of work into a project update? What details do they filter out, and what do they consider critical?
* RQ4 (Agent Delegation Triggers): What tasks do users actually want to hand off versus what they want to keep control over? What parameters (triggers, boundaries, outputs) define a successful Sojo Agent assignment?
Session script
Artifact & Task Audit (15 mins)
* Goal: Uncover the hidden, informal task ecosystems.
* Activity: Have them open their laptop and show you their current "active stack."
* Prompts to use:
   * "Show me the 3 things you know you have to do today but aren't written down in a formal project board."
   * "Look at your open browser tabs or flagged emails right now—what tasks do those represent?"
   * "Where is the conversation happening about your top priority right now? Can you show me that Slack thread/email chain?"
The "Human Ollie" Walkthrough (25 mins)
* Goal: Treat the user's memory and screen as the database to see what Ollie should have done.
* Activity: Walk backward through their last 24–48 hours of work. Have them literally point at things on their screen.
* Prompts to use:
   * "You opened this Google Doc at 10 AM and left a comment. If Ollie was a smart assistant watching this, what is the 'core project update' it should extract from that action?"
   * "If an AI wrote an update based purely on this Slack thread you just showed me, what would it miss? What's the unwritten context here?"
   * "Look at this completed task. What were all the scattered pieces of data (spreadsheets, links, client feedback) that were required to get it done?"
AI Agents tasks (15 mins)
* Goal: Use their real data to define the scope and boundaries of the agents.
* Activity: Review the real tasks identified in Phase 1 and 2. Print out blank "Sojo Agent Job Description" cards (or use a digital whiteboard) and fill them out together.
* Prompts to use:
   * "Look at this specific task you spent an hour on yesterday. If an AI Agent could have done it, what is the exact trigger to start it? (e.g., 'When a new CSV lands in this folder' or 'When a client pings me via email')."
   * "What does the agent need access to on your laptop to successfully do this?"
   * "What is the exact output you expect? A draft email? A spreadsheet update? And where should it put it?"

Attendees: Cowboy Lynk Sascha Negele Mike Carcasole Zhengrong Ji Ian Mendiola Belinda Preno Michelle Haq Mirjam van Esch Kishan Dedakia Simon Sok


Top of Minds
* Last week’s sprint

      * Infers tasks based on context and connectors
      * Assigns to teammates or agents

* Goal this week: Get app in a good state so we can (a) Dogfood (b) Be the most effective at the sprint next week

Sprint

Updates
* Eng

* Product

* Design

* Aiming to have an updated version of the prototype so Zhengrong can share with CT June 22
* UXR: 
   * Unmoderated survey and video responses (~90 ppl) https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/10PLeRSRBFKLA26X5RSrF6CsGG8mEJIrD9OwaUu3bYCA/edit?slide=id.g3eaea0c1acc_0_7498#slide=id.g3eaea0c1acc_0_7498
      * Projects frequently originate in live meetings or email/chat communications.
      * Workers prefer asynchronous methods like chat or email to clarify vague tasks.
      * Shared documents are the primary environment for actual collaborative work.
      * Task tracking is often manual, fragmented across tools, or reliant on memory
      * Progress updates are typically manual and ad-hoc, often depending on informal syncs
      * Teams predominantly perform manual verification of AI-generated content instead of using automation.
   * Come up with agenda/plan for UXR by June 15
      * Which type of profile are we looking to recruit
         * PGM (Charles?)
         * Operations (teams that work on short term project for other teams, like UXI, HR, Rews)
         * And i guess really anyone we can get


   * Next hosting interviews tomorrow and monday


:

Jun 9, 2026 | Project Friday daily sync
Attendees: Cowboy Lynk Sascha Negele Mike Carcasole Zhengrong Ji Ian Mendiola Simon Sok Belinda Preno Michelle Haq Mirjam van Esch Gabor Cselle


Discussion
* Your inferred projects and tasks; configuration and reliability
* Single vs multiplayer
* Current friction points:
   * Keeping tasks up to date is work, though the agent makes it a bit easier
   * I can only delegate a subset of tasks to an agent




Updates
* Eng
* Product
* Design
   * Homepage Update (Suggestions, feed)
   * Zero state (start with stream)
* UXR (cannot join in person because of summit, uxr updates and asks below)


This week survey and interviews:
We have acquired the budget for the following screening, survey, and interview plans.
We are kicking of a DScout (UXR platform) project.
* We aim to send out screener today. The screener identifies eligible participants (employed and collaborate with others as part of their role. We will select participants across segment, roles and screen for good quality respondents)
Ask: review screener.. Sending out screener right after this meeting. 
* Next, we will collect 100 survey responses from participants selected in the screening—comprising multiple-choice, open-ended, and video inputs—regarding current workflows for initiating and scoping collaboration projects, tracking tasks, updating collaborators, and using AI in team-based tasks.
Ask: review the survey
Sending out survey (to any screened on participants) as soon as possible. 
* Finally, we will invite 20-50 of the survey respondents for a deep-dive 30-minute interview, tentatively scheduled for Friday, June 12th, and Monday, June 15th. but we should monitor feasibility based on availability and survey fill rate. Ask should we reserve some of the interviews to host post summit concept feedback testing 
                
Next week Recruiting onsite participants: Since the timeline is too tight to bring in external customers, we should focus on recruiting Googlers instead.
Ask Please let me know what specific profile we are targeting so I can begin the recruitment process.

________________




Jun 8, 2026 | [:35] Project Friday daily sync
Attendees: Cowboy Lynk Sascha Negele Mike Carcasole Zhengrong Ji Ian Mendiola Simon Sok Belinda Preno Gabor Cselle Michelle Haq Mirjam van Esch




   * Gabor
   * User research sprint

   * 3 day sprint next week, hands on with real users

________________




Jun 4, 2026 | Project Friday daily sync
Attendees: Belinda Preno Cowboy Lynk Ian Mendiola Zhengrong Ji Mike Carcasole Sascha Negele Simon Sok
